# EX09: Data Analysis for Continuous Improvement

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="C:\Users\hmara\.vscode\comp110-26s-workspace\personal-site.github.io\static\imgs\logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

**Author:** Sakethram Maramraj
**Course:** COMP 110

## Summary
Most students who take COMP110 come in with little to no coding experience, but the course moves at the same pace for everyone. I wanted to see if that's actually a problem: **do beginners find the course noticeably harder or harder to keep up with compared to students who already had some experience going in?**

To test this, I used survey responses from over 700 COMP110 students and looked at how they rated the course's difficulty, pace, and their own understanding, grouped by how much prior programming experience they had. The data showed a pretty clear pattern. Students with no prior experience consistently rated the course as harder and felt they understood the material less than students who came in with even just a few months of background. The gap was visible whether you looked at the average numbers directly or the charts.

This suggests it might be worth giving experienced students an optional faster track. This is not to exclude beginners, but so instructors can focus more of their support on the majority who are learning to code for the first time.

## Charts
<img src="C:\Users\hmara\.vscode\comp110-26s-workspace\personal-site.github.io\static\imgs\chart1.png" alt="Bar chart showing the distribution of prior programming experience among 764 COMP110 students, with the majority having no prior experience." width="600"/>

<img src="C:\Users\hmara\.vscode\comp110-26s-workspace\personal-site.github.io\static\imgs\chart2.png" alt="Box plot comparing course difficulty ratings across prior experience groups, showing beginners tend to rate the course as harder." width="600"/>

<img src="C:\Users\hmara\.vscode\comp110-26s-workspace\personal-site.github.io\static\imgs\chart3.png" alt="Scatter plot of understanding vs. difficulty ratings colored by prior experience group, showing beginners cluster toward higher difficulty and lower understanding." width="600"/>

<img src="C:\Users\hmara\.vscode\comp110-26s-workspace\personal-site.github.io\static\imgs\chart4.png" alt="Bar chart of average understanding ratings by prior experience group, showing understanding increases as prior experience increases." width="600"/>

## Conclusion
The data generally supported the idea. Students with no prior programming experience rated the course as harder and reported lower understanding than students who came in with even a small amount of background. This pattern was consistent across all the charts. Beginners averaged a difficulty rating of 4.56 out of 7 compared to 4.11 for students with 2–6 months of experience, and their average understanding score was lower (4.01 vs. 4.38). While the differences aren't crazy large, they're consistent enough to suggest that a one-size-fits-all pace may not be ideal for everyone.
